1.pericope_label: The Lord Our God Is Holy
1.synthesis: “The LORD reigns; let the peoples tremble!… The LORD is great in Zion; he is exalted over all the peoples” (vv.1–2). Three times the psalm declares God holy — “Holy is he!… Holy is he!… the LORD our God is holy!” (v.3, v.5, v.9). It recalls that Moses, Aaron, and Samuel called on him and he answered, “a forgiving God… but an avenger of their wrongdoings” (vv.6–8). The integrative thread is the holiness of the reigning King who both forgives and judges: the enthronement psalms climax in a threefold proclamation of God’s holiness, summoning the peoples to tremble and worship the holy LORD who answered his servants’ prayers and is both merciful and just.
1.historical_context: Psalm 99 completes the enthronement group with its threefold “holy” (v.3, v.5, v.9), echoing the seraphic “Holy, holy, holy” (Isaiah 6:3). God enthroned “upon the cherubim” (v.1) recalls the ark. The appeal to Moses, Aaron, and Samuel as intercessors who were answered (v.6) grounds the call to worship in God’s proven readiness to hear, even as he both forgave and judged (v.8).
1.literary_structure: The psalm is structured by the threefold refrain of God’s holiness (v.3, v.5, v.9), each followed by a call to exalt and worship. The central section on the praying intercessors (vv.6–8) anchors the abstract holiness in God’s relational faithfulness, balancing transcendence and nearness.
1.theological_application: The reigning God is supremely holy, and his holiness calls the peoples to tremble and worship (v.1, v.3; Hebrews 12:28–29). Yet this holy King hears his servants’ prayers and is “a forgiving God,” even while he judges sin (v.8) — holiness and mercy held together. The believer learns to approach God with reverent awe, confident that the thrice-holy LORD both pardons the penitent and upholds his justice, and that he answers those who call upon him. The reigning God is supremely holy, and his holiness calls the peoples to tremble and worship (v.1, v.3; Hebrews 12:28–29). Yet this holy King hears his servants’ prayers and is “a forgiving God,” even while he judges sin (v.8) — holiness and mercy held together. The believer approaches God with reverent awe, confident that the thrice-holy LORD both pardons the penitent and upholds his justice, and answers all who call upon him in truth.
1.christology: The thrice-holy LORD (v.3, v.5, v.9; Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8) is the God now known as Father, Son, and Spirit. The holiness that bids the peoples tremble is reconciled with his forgiving mercy (v.8) in Christ, in whom God is both “just and the justifier” (Romans 3:26). The great Intercessors of old (v.6) foreshadow the one Mediator who ever lives to intercede (Hebrews 7:25), through whom the holy God hears and forgives his people.
